explain.rule: Use 'will' to talk about the future — predictions, promises, decisions made at the moment of speaking, and offers. Form: subject + will + base verb. Negative: will not (won't) + base verb. Question: Will + subject + base verb?
explain.full_explanation: Will is used in several key situations: (1) Predictions about the future, especially opinions and beliefs: 'I think it will rain tomorrow.' (2) Decisions made at the moment of speaking, not planned before: 'I'll have the pasta, please.' (3) Promises: 'I will call you later.' (4) Offers and offers of help: 'I'll carry that for you.' (5) Threats or warnings: 'If you do that again, I will tell the teacher.' The key contrast is with 'going to' (G-06) — will is for spontaneous decisions and general predictions, going to is for plans already made.

positive: Subject + will + base verb → I will come, She will help, They will arrive
Contracted form (common in speech): I'll, you'll, he'll, she'll, it'll, we'll, they'll
negative: Subject + will not + base verb → I will not come, She will not help
Contracted: won't → I won't come, She won't help
question: Will + subject + base verb? → Will you come? Will she help?
short_answers: Yes, I will. / No, I won't.
Yes, she will. / No, she won't.

when_to_use: Predictions based on opinion: I think England will win the match.
Spontaneous decisions at the moment of speaking: 'The phone is ringing.' 'I'll get it!'
Promises: I promise I will never lie to you again.
Offers of help: Don't worry, I'll help you move.
Requests: Will you help me with this?
Threats/warnings: If you're late again, I will dock your pay.
explain.why_matters: Will is one of the most common ways to express the future in English. It appears constantly in everyday conversation, especially for quick decisions and promises. The contracted forms (I'll, she'll, won't) are used in almost all spoken English.
explain.hebrew_note: Hebrew expresses the future using a dedicated future tense form of the verb — 'אני אלך', 'היא תבוא'. English uses will + base verb for the same purpose. One key difference: in Hebrew, the future tense is built into the verb form. In English, 'will' never changes form regardless of the subject — I will, she will, they will — all the same. Also, the contracted forms (I'll, she'll) can sound like just a tiny L sound in fast speech — listen carefully.
explain.tip: Won't = will not. This contraction is extremely common. Remember: will NEVER changes for different subjects. There is no 'he wills' or 'she wills'. Always: will + base verb.

good_examples.sentence: I'll call you when I arrive.
good_examples.audio_text: I'll call you when I arrive.
good_examples.note: Promise — contraction I'll in natural speech

good_examples.sentence: She will probably be late — the traffic is terrible.
good_examples.audio_text: She will probably be late — the traffic is terrible.

good_examples.sentence: Will you help me carry these bags?
good_examples.audio_text: Will you help me carry these bags?

good_examples.sentence: I won't forget your birthday this year, I promise.
good_examples.audio_text: I won't forget your birthday this year, I promise.
The printer is broken. I'll fix it.
good_examples.note: Spontaneous decision made at the moment of speaking

good_examples.sentence: It will be cold tomorrow, so take a jacket.
good_examples.audio_text: It will be cold tomorrow, so take a jacket.

good_examples.sentence: Will there be enough food for everyone?
good_examples.audio_text: Will there be enough food for everyone?

good_examples.sentence: Don't worry, everything will be fine.
good_examples.audio_text: Don't worry, everything will be fine.

bad_examples.wrong: I will to call you later.
bad_examples.reason: After will, use base verb — never 'to + verb'
Will never adds -s for he/she/it — it is always just 'will'

bad_examples.wrong: I will going to the shop.
bad_examples.correct: I will go to the shop.
bad_examples.reason: After will, use the base verb — not -ing, not going to

exercises.question: I promise I ___ (call) you as soon as I get home.
exercises.hint: Promise → will + base verb
exercises.explanation: Will + call. Promises always use will. Never 'will to call'.
exercises.hebrew_note: הבטחה באנגלית: I will call — will + פועל בצורת הבסיס

exercises.question: 'I'm cold.' 'I ___ (close) the window for you.'
exercises.hint: Spontaneous decision → will + base verb
exercises.explanation: Spontaneous offer/decision at the moment of speaking uses will. I'll close = I will close.

exercises.question: ___ (you / help) me with my homework later?
exercises.hint: Request → Will + subject + base verb
exercises.explanation: Will + you + help. Request about future action.

exercises.question: She ___ (not / come) to the party — she has other plans.
exercises.hint: Negative → won't + base verb
exercises.explanation: Won't + come. Won't = will not. Base verb stays as 'come'.

exercises.question: I think it ___ (rain) this afternoon — look at those dark clouds.
exercises.hint: Prediction based on evidence → will + base verb
exercises.explanation: Will + rain. Prediction based on what you can see (dark clouds).

exercises.question: Don't worry — everything ___ (be) fine, I'm sure.
exercises.hint: Reassurance / prediction → will + base verb be
exercises.explanation: Will + be. Future state prediction using the base verb 'be'.

exercises.question: ___ (there / be) enough seats for everyone?
exercises.hint: Question about future existence → Will + there + be
exercises.explanation: Will + there + be. 'There will be' in statement form becomes 'Will there be?' in question form.

exercises.question: He ___ (probably / not / arrive) before seven.
exercises.answer: probably won't arrive / will probably not arrive
exercises.hint: 'Probably' goes between will and the verb, or before won't
exercises.explanation: Will probably not arrive / probably won't arrive. Probability adverbs go between will and the verb.

exercises.question: I ___ (carry) that for you — it looks very heavy.
exercises.hint: Offer of help → will + base verb
exercises.explanation: Spontaneous offer: will + carry.

exercises.question: Scientists believe that temperatures ___ (rise) significantly in the next century.
exercises.hint: Long-term prediction → will + base verb
exercises.explanation: Will + rise. Future prediction about something inevitable or very likely.

exercises.question: ___ (she / be) at the conference next week?
exercises.hint: Question about future → Will + subject + base verb be
exercises.explanation: Will + she + be. Future question.

exercises.question: I ___ (not / tell) anyone your secret — I promise.
exercises.hint: Promise (negative) → won't + base verb
exercises.explanation: Won't + tell. A promise not to do something.

exercises.question: 'Oh no, I've forgotten my wallet!' 'Don't worry, ___.'
exercises.hint: Spontaneous offer → which form expresses this?
exercises.explanation: I'll pay = I will pay. This is a spontaneous decision/offer made at the moment of realising the problem.
distractors: I'm going to pay for you

exercises.question: I think the exam ___ difficult.
exercises.hint: Opinion about the future → which form?
exercises.explanation: Will be — expressing an opinion/prediction about the future. 'I think' + will is a classic combination.

exercises.question: She ___ be a doctor one day — she is so dedicated.
exercises.hint: Prediction about future career
exercises.explanation: Will — prediction about the future based on present qualities.

exercises.question: Which sentence contains a promise?
exercises.answer: I will never lie to you again.
exercises.hint: Which one sounds like a firm commitment about the future?
exercises.explanation: 'I will never lie to you again' is a promise — a firm commitment about future behaviour.
distractors: I am going to the shop tomorrow.

exercises.question: Which form is WRONG?
exercises.hint: Will never changes for he/she/it
exercises.explanation: Will never adds -s. It is always 'will' for all subjects. 'She wills' is incorrect.

exercises.question: The correct negative contraction of 'will not' is:
exercises.hint: This is an irregular contraction — it doesn't follow the usual pattern
exercises.explanation: Won't = will not. This is an irregular contraction — notice the vowel changes. Not 'willn't' or 'won't' with different spelling.

exercises.question: Find and fix the mistake: 'I will to call you tomorrow morning.'
exercises.answer: I will call you tomorrow morning.
exercises.hint: What comes after will?
exercises.explanation: Will + base verb. Never 'will + to + verb'. The 'to' is not used after modal verbs like will.
exercises.hebrew_note: בעברית לפעמים מרגיש טבעי לומר 'to' אחרי will, אבל זה שגוי — will + פועל בסיסי בלבד

exercises.question: Find and fix the mistake: 'She wills probably arrive late.'
exercises.hint: Does 'will' ever change for he/she/it?
exercises.explanation: Will never adds -s. All subjects use 'will'. Probably goes between will and the main verb.

exercises.question: Find and fix the mistake: 'Will you to help me with this?'
exercises.answer: Will you help me with this?
exercises.hint: After will in a question, what form is the verb?
exercises.explanation: Will + subject + base verb. Never 'will + to + verb'.

exercises.question: Find and fix the mistake: 'I won't to tell anyone, I promise.'
exercises.answer: I won't tell anyone, I promise.
exercises.hint: Won't + base verb — no 'to'
exercises.explanation: Won't + base verb. No 'to' after any modal verb.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.hint: Subject + will + base verb + object + time
exercises.explanation: I + will + call + you + later.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: She won't come to the party.
exercises.hint: Subject + won't + base verb + to + place
exercises.explanation: She + won't + come + to the party.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: I think our team will win.
exercises.hint: Opinion + prediction: I think + subject + will + base verb
exercises.explanation: I think + our team (subject) + will + win (base verb).

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: Will you help me with that?
exercises.hint: Request question: Will + subject + base verb + objects
exercises.explanation: Will + you + help + me + with that?

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.hint: Weather prediction: It + will + be + adjective + time
exercises.explanation: It + will + be + cold + tomorrow. Weather predictions use 'it' as the subject.

speak.sentence: I'll call you as soon as the meeting is over.
chunks: as soon as the meeting is over.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'I'll' sounds like just 'I' + a quick L at the end. In natural speech it is very short. 'Meeting' = MEET-ing. 'Over' = OH-ver.

speak.sentence: Don't worry, everything will be fine in the end.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Everything' = EV-ree-thing. Three syllables. 'Won't' = WOHNT. 'Fine' — the I sounds like in 'my'.

speak.sentence: Will you be at home this evening? I'd like to come over.
chunks: Will you be at home this evening?
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Evening' = EEV-ning. Two syllables. 'I'd like' = I would like. Contract quickly: AYD-like.

speak.sentence: I won't forget to bring your book back, I promise.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Won't' = WOHNT — rhymes with 'don't'. 'Forget' = fer-GET. Stress on second syllable. 'Promise' = PROM-is.

speak.sentence: The weather forecast says it will rain all weekend.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Forecast' = FOR-kast. Stress on first syllable. 'Weekend' = WEEK-end. 'Rain' — long A sound as in 'cake'.

speak.sentence: I think she will get the promotion — she works incredibly hard.
chunks: I think she will get the promotion
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Promotion' = pruh-MOH-shun. Stress on second syllable. 'Incredibly' = in-KRED-ib-lee. Four syllables.

speak.sentence: Shall I open the window? It will get cooler in here.
chunks: It will get cooler in here.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Shall' = SHAL — formal/British alternative to 'will' for I/we. 'Cooler' = KOO-ler. 'Window' = WIN-doh.

speak.sentence: Scientists predict that sea levels will rise by two metres by the end of the century.
chunks: that sea levels will rise by two metres
by the end of the century.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Scientists' = SY-un-tists. Three syllables. 'Century' = SEN-chuh-ree. 'Metres' = MEE-terz (British) or MEE-terz.
